title: Issue starting rootless container with systemd
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When attempting to control autostart of a rootless container with systemd, it errors out with a message of 
`error creating libpod runtime: database libpod temporary files directory (tmpdir) \"/run/user/1000/run\" does not match our libpod temporary files directory (tmpdir) \"/var/run/libpod\": database  configuration mismatch"`

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a rootless container

2. podman generate systemd container > /lib/systemd/system/pod-container.service

3. sudo systemctl start pod-container.service

**Describe the results you received:**
Service fails to start.

[systemctl_status.txt](https://github.com/containers/libpod/files/4697950/systemctl_status.txt)
[journalctl.txt](https://github.com/containers/libpod/files/4697923/journalctl.txt)

**Describe the results you expected:**
The container should start normally

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.9.1
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.14.2
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

    name: crun
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
Can you upgrade to Podman v1.9.2 or higher and re-try? We've already fixed this one.

I don't know if v1.9.2 every made it to stable in Fedora, but v1.9.3 should be in updates-testing - `dnf --enablerepo=updates-testing upgrade podman` should grab it.

Sorry for not replying Immediately, but this is NOT resolved.
I tried with Podman 1.9.2, same issue, then I upgraded to the testing 1.9.3, and the issue still persists. Please re-open.

It does seem to be throwing a different error now though:
`Error: unable to find container 952a8f7a8a602a83ca192c4a608da95412e5a9f6ff4c5571bfed5fe2d410ff6b: no container with name or ID 952a8f7a8a602a83ca192c4a608da95412e5a9f6ff4c5571bfed5fe2d410ff6b found: no such container`

However I have verified that the container ID has not changed, and I can start it manually by calling `podman start 952a8f7a8a602a83ca192c4a608da95412e5a9f6ff4c5571bfed5fe2d410ff6b`

Root cannot see rootless containers as the storage paths differ. When running rootless containers via systemd, the unit files must be installed at the correct path (see https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/docs/source/markdown/podman-generate-systemd.1.md#installation-of-generated-systemd-unit-files) and be started via `systemctl **--user**`.

> Root cannot see rootless containers as the storage paths differ. When running rootless containers via systemd, the unit files must be installed at the correct path (see https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/docs/source/markdown/podman-generate-systemd.1.md#installation-of-generated-systemd-unit-files) and be started via `systemctl **--user**`.

The link your reference seems to contradict your statement.

`Note: Coping unit files to /usr/lib/systemd/system and enabling it marks the unit file to be automatically started at boot. And smillarly, coping a unit file to $HOME/.config/systemd/user and enabling it marks the unit file to be automatically started on user login`

I need these containers to start at boot, so I place the config in the appropriate path. How else am I supposed to securely start non root containers at boot?

The problem in your specific example is:

> podman generate systemd container > /lib/systemd/system/pod-container.service

The generated .service file _starts_ an existing container which is in the rootless storage. Hence, you cannot use it as another user (even root) which ultimately leads to 

> Error: unable to find container 952a8f7a8a602a83ca192c4a608da95412e5a9f6ff4c5571bfed5fe2d410ff6b: no container with name or ID 952a8f7a8a602a83ca192c4a608da95412e5a9f6ff4c5571bfed5fe2d410ff6b found: no such container

... as root container storage does not know this container.

Running rootless container in system (i.e., root) services can be done by user the `User/Group=...` attributes of systemd. An example can be found in the following issue: https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/5572#issuecomment-626727010

However, there are _many_ things that could go wrong and we don't yet officially support it. But we're working together with the systemd folks to find a solution.
I recommend using `podman generate systemd --new` which will generate services that create _new_ container in contrast to the default services which start existing containers.

Will the ` podman generate systemd --new` generated file in /lib/systemd start as root or as user? The whole point is to run the containers as non-root.

I will try your other suggestion of supplying the user/group to the systemd file. Maybe this should be reopened as a feature enhancment then? Surely I'm not the only one who would like to see rootless containers auto start at boot up.

> Will the podman generate systemd --new generated file in /lib/systemd start as root or as user? The whole point is to run the containers as non-root.

They will start with all users. But note that you want to run rootless containers as root (via a root system service).

> I will try your other suggestion of supplying the user/group to the systemd file. 

Please note that we don't support that yet (see #5572).

> Maybe this should be reopened as a feature enhancment then? Surely I'm not the only one who would like to see rootless containers auto start at boot up.

This is already supported. You need to enable lingering (`# loginctl enable-linger username`) for your user and enable the _non-root_ service via `$ systemctl --user $service`.

`Systemctl - - user $service` does NOT enable them at boot, it only enables them at login. Since this is a server, if there happens to be A failure where it needs to reboot, they will not start until the user logs in. So it appears to not be supported yet.

Again. Your scenario is supported but you need to enable lingering for your user. I just created a clean Fedora 32 VM and did the following steps:

1. Create a .service file and copy it to `$HOME/.config/systemd/user`.
2. Load and enable the service via `systemctl --user ...`
3. Enable lingering via: `# loginctl enable-linger username`
4. Reboot the system.
5. Login as root (not as the user) and check if the service is running (and it is running).

Step 3 is the crucial part of getting the services to start at boot (even for non root users).
Sorry for dragging this out. I kept getting confused by the constant statement that it wasn't supported. (And it was early)
No worries, this is a quite complex setup :+1:
